planting.note: Plant container or bare-root roses in early spring or fall in slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Knock Out requires a minimum of 6 hours of direct sun daily for best bloom production — in less than 6 hours, flowering quantity decreases significantly.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun (minimum 6 hours direct sun); the original Knock Out rose ('Radrazz') fundamentally transformed landscape rose culture when it was introduced in 2000 — a disease-resistant, self-cleaning, repeat-blooming shrub rose that produces cherry-red semi-double flowers continuously from late spring through hard frost with no deadheading required and outstanding resistance to black spot and powdery mildew. Where traditional roses require weekly spraying, careful deadheading, and precise fertilization, Knock Out needs only annual pruning, occasional water, and periodic fertilization. It blooms in flushes approximately every 5–6 weeks. Prune to 12–18 inches in late winter before new growth begins — cutting hard promotes vigorous new flowering stems. In zone 9, it blooms from March through December and may be semi-evergreen. Feed with a balanced rose fertilizer or slow-release granular 2–3 times per growing season. The original single-red form; related cultivars in the Knock Out family include Double Knock Out (fuller flowers), Pink Knock Out, Rainbow Knock Out, and Blushing Knock Out.
sources: All-America Rose Selections — Knock Out 2000 winner

planting.note: Plant container roses in early spring or fall in slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Pink Knock Out shares all the low-maintenance attributes of the original red Knock Out — self-cleaning, disease-resistant, repeat-blooming — but in soft pink tones that complement mixed border plantings.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Pink Knock Out is a member of the breakthrough Knock Out rose family, sharing the original cultivar's extraordinary disease resistance, self-cleaning habit, and continuous bloom production but with medium-pink semi-double flowers that are lighter and softer than the cherry-red original. The pastel pink color blends naturally with both warm and cool companion plants, making it more versatile for mixed plantings than the strong red. It blooms from late spring through frost in flushes every 5–6 weeks, requires no deadheading, and shows outstanding resistance to black spot — the most common and damaging rose disease in humid climates. Prune hard (to 12–18 inches) in late winter; light selective pruning during the season is optional but not required. In zone 9, apply 2–3 inches of mulch over the root zone to prevent moisture loss and reduce root zone temperature stress in the peak summer heat.

common_name: Iceberg Rose (Schneewittchen Rose, Fée des Neiges)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container roses in early spring in slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), well-drained, fertile, amended soil in full sun (minimum 6 hours); Iceberg is among the most widely planted floribunda roses in the world and performs exceptionally well in warm climates including the Gulf Coast.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile, amended soil in full sun; Iceberg is one of the most celebrated and widely grown roses of the 20th century — a floribunda introduced in Germany in 1958 that has been continuously popular for over 60 years for its consistent performance, clean appearance, and prolific production of pure white, lightly fragrant flowers in large clusters throughout the growing season. The flowers are held above glossy, light-green foliage in a clean, attractive arrangement, and the plant shows better disease resistance than many classic floribundas. Iceberg performs excellently in warm climates and is one of the best white roses for zone 8–9. It blooms in flushes through the season, is effectively self-cleaning, and recovers well from pruning. The climbing form ('Climbing Iceberg') is a sport that can reach 12–15 feet on a fence or arbor and is one of the best white climbing roses. Prune bushy plants to 12–18 inches in late winter; cut climbing forms lightly to shape immediately after the main spring bloom.
sources: World Federation of Rose Societies — Rose Hall of Fame

common_name: New Dawn Rose (Ever-Blooming Doctor Van Fleet)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container roses in late winter or early spring against a sturdy support structure; New Dawn requires strong architectural support — a substantial fence, arbor, or pergola capable of supporting a mature plant that can weigh several hundred pounds.

directions: Plant against a sturdy support in slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; New Dawn holds the distinction of being the first plant ever patented in the United States (Plant Patent No. 1, 1931) and remains one of the most beloved climbing roses in cultivation, producing masses of medium-pink, lightly fragrant, semi-double flowers on long, arching canes that can reach 15–20 feet. New Dawn is a sport of 'Dr. Van Fleet' that gained its fame partly by blooming repeatedly rather than once in spring — an unusual quality in vigor climbing roses of its era. The silvery-pink flowers fade gradually to nearly white and are produced in large clusters in spring with reliable repeat bloom in fall. The vigorous, arching canes with glossy, disease-resistant foliage require substantial annual tying and training to maintain the climbing habit; left unsupported it forms a large sprawling shrub. New Dawn tolerates partial shade better than most climbing roses, making it valuable for training on north-facing or partially shaded walls and fences.
sources: World Federation of Rose Societies — Rose Hall of Fame

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container roses in late winter or early spring in slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), well-amended, deeply prepared, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Mr. Lincoln requires the dedicated care typical of hybrid tea roses — consistent watering, fertilizing, and pest management — but produces some of the finest, most fragrant flowers of any rose.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, well-amended, deeply prepared, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Mr. Lincoln is one of the most fragrant and most widely grown deep-red hybrid tea roses in American horticulture, introduced in 1964 and still listed as one of the best red roses for fragrance by the American Rose Society. The flowers are classic hybrid tea form — high-centered, deeply cupped, velvety dark crimson-red — with an exceptional old-rose fragrance that is among the strongest of any modern rose. The upright canes reach 4–6 feet with dark, glossy foliage. Like all hybrid tea roses, Mr. Lincoln requires more care than modern landscape roses — it is susceptible to black spot and needs regular preventative spraying, consistent fertilization, and precise watering. The reward is extraordinary: no other rose type produces flowers of such classical elegance and overwhelming fragrance. In zone 9, provide deep mulch and consistent irrigation; the plant may go semi-dormant in peak summer heat but returns to bloom in fall.

common_name: Peace Rose (Gloria Dei, Mme. A. Meilland)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container roses in late winter or early spring in slightly acidic, well-amended, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Peace is vigorous and more disease-resistant than many hybrid tea roses, but benefits from preventative fungicide application and consistent feeding in humid climates.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, well-amended, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Peace rose holds a unique place in horticultural history — introduced during World War II and officially named at the San Francisco United Nations conference in 1945, it is considered by many the most famous rose of the 20th century. The large, high-centered flowers are distinguished by their remarkable color combination: soft yellow petals edged with carmine-pink that intensifies with age, creating an unusually warm, layered flower. It is more vigorous and disease-resistant than most hybrid teas of its era, with large, glossy, dark-green foliage. Peace is still widely grown and performs reliably in zone 4–9 with standard hybrid tea care. The story of its distribution during World War II — with cuttings smuggled out of France before the Nazi occupation — and its formal naming at the historic UN conference make it one of the most culturally significant plants ever grown. Prune hard in late winter; feed with rose fertilizer monthly through the growing season.
sources: World Federation of Rose Societies — Rose Hall of Fame
